title: Speculum Sophicum Rhodostauroticum: The Mirror of Rosicrucian Wisdom
subtitle: Theophilus Schweighardt (Daniel Mogling)'s 1617 unveiling of the Collegium Fraternitatis — the inner quest for the Rose Cross through ORA et LABORA, the Theologia-Physiologia syzygy, and the building without windows or doors

summary.one_line: Schweighardt's 'Mirror of the Wisdom of the Rosicrucians' — the symbolic unveiling of the inner Collegium, the polarities of Physiologia and Theologia, and the instruction that the true Rosicrucian...
summary.scope: The complete text of Schweighardt's 1617 Speculum Sophicum Rhodostauroticum, including the title-page symbolism (ORA and LABORA, Physiologia and Theologia, the rose-cross with wreath), the three chapters on the Collegium, its statutes, and the seven axioms. Covers the central Rosicrucian doctrine that the fraternity is invisible and accessed through inner transformation.

sources.label: Speculum Sophicum Rhodostauroticum — Theophilus Schweighardt (Daniel Mogling), 1617, translated by Donald Maclean

body.text: Two female figures are set in niches one labelled PHYSIOLOGIA and the other THEOLOGIA. Physiologia who represents the spiritual transformative forces working in the human soul from out of our bodily nature holds a winged flaming heart in her right hand and three symbols — a Rose, a feather quill pen, and a latin cross, while above her is the statement 'This I hold for my radiant joy.' Her syzygy Theologia, representing the spiritual force working from above into the human soul, holds a heart bearing the first and last things, the Alpha and Omega, A and Z, Aleph and Tau, the alchemical AZOTH, while in her left hand she holds a ruler and compass labelled 'The Art of Nature,' and above her is written 'God's word which remains in eternity.' Between them, a panel with a cross surrounded with a wreath of roses bears the words 'I am practiced in this.'

There is a building, a great building lacking windows and doors, a princely, aye imperial palace, everywhere visible, but hidden from the eyes of men, adorned with all kinds of divine and natural things, the contemplation of which in theory and practice is granted to every man free of charge and remuneration, but heeded by few because the building appears as bad, little worth, old and well-known to the mind of the mob who are ever heedless and seekers after things new; but the building itself is so precious, so delicate, artistic and wonderful in its construction that no wealth, gold, jewel, money, goods, honour, authority or reputation in the whole world can be named which is not to be found in that high reputable palace in high degree. It is itself so strongly fortified by God and nature, and preserved against the onslaught of the ignorant, that even though all the mines, cannon, battering-rams and petards and such recently invented military devices were used against it all human endeavour and toil would be useless and in vain. This is the Collegium ad S.S. of the Rosicrucian Brotherhood, this is the royal, nay more than imperial palace of which the brethren in their 'Fama' make mention.

Know therefore, brother who lovest God and art, that according to the announcement of the brethren although the incorporated gathering of all Rosicrucians does not take place in one particular place, nevertheless a true-hearted, devout and upright man can easily and without great trouble come to speak with one of the brethren; I say a devout and upright man, but not a gross and high-faluting Thraso, a gold-greedy Ardelio, or a worldly-wise Authophilus. Seek not, in vain is all thy toil, mark now what I inform thee here, if thou doest it, and followest my teaching, a brother will soon be with thee. Write not, thou hast then proved thyself, with prayer hast been admitted to the school. I beg thee for God's and thine own salvation's and advantage's sake, only follow, thou shalt not regret it. It is not necessary that thou go into much danger, for it must be a mean place where no brother has been within four weeks. Only be calm, still, place thy hope in God, pray unto him without ceasing, hearken unto and read diligently his word and ponder it in thy heart. From my heart I speak: go into thyself, put all worldly things behind thee.

The pairing of Physiologia and Theologia as two complementary forces — one rising from below (the winged flaming heart, the rose and cross of immanent transformation) and one descending from above (the Alpha and Omega, the compass and ruler of transcendent wisdom) — is the alchemical formulation of the Tantric pair Shakti and Shiva. Physiologia is the shakti that works 'from out of our bodily nature,' the kundalini that rises from the muladhara, bearing the 'winged flaming heart' (the awakened anahata, where the serpent reaches the heart-lotus). The three symbols — Rose, quill, cross — are the tattvas of sattva (the rose), rajas (the quill of creative expression), and tamas (the cross of material embodiment). Theologia is Shiva, the transcendent consciousness that descends 'from above,' bearing the AZOTH (the alpha and omega, the beginning and end of all tattvas) and the ruler and compass of cosmic law (the rita, the cosmic order of the Rig Veda). 'I am practiced in this' — written on the cross surrounded by roses — is the response of the yogi who has unified the two currents: the ascent of shakti and the descent of grace. In Steiner's Rosicrucian path, this is the 'union of the Macrocosm and Microcosm,' where the lower forces are purified into the higher ones and the higher ones 'rain down' into the purified soul.

Schweighardt's invisible Collegium — a building without windows or doors that is 'everywhere visible, but hidden from the eyes of men' — is the alchemical image of what the Buddhist tradition calls the sangha: the invisible community of those on the path, which is not an external organization but a spiritual constellation that becomes accessible through inner transformation. In the Tantraloka (13.115-125), this is the 'kula' — the family of initiates who are connected through shakti-pata (the descent of grace) rather than through physical meeting. The statement that it is 'so strongly fortified by God and nature' that no external force can breach it echoes the Upanishadic teaching that the brahma-pura (the city of Brahman in the heart) is guarded by the 'doors of the senses' that only the yogi can open through meditation. The 'mean place where no brother has been within four weeks' is any location where the disciple has not yet turned within — for the brother (the daimon, the inner guide) is always present wherever the disciple is, but is only 'met' when the disciple has achieved the requisite inner stillness. In Steiner's account, this is the Guardian of the Threshold, who appears only when the disciple is ready, and who is in truth the disciple's own higher self.

Schweighardt's instruction — 'go into thyself, put all worldly things behind thee' — is the universal counsel of the sadhana (spiritual practice) that the Layayoga calls pratyahara (withdrawal of the senses) and dharana (concentration). His insistence that the seeker need not travel or seek external teachers — that 'it is not necessary that thou go into much danger, for it must be a mean place where no brother has been within four weeks' — is the same teaching that the Avadhuta Gita gives: the guru is within, and the physical teacher is only an occasion for the awakening of the inner daimon. The 'calm, still, hope in God' that he prescribes is the state of sharanagati (surrender) that in the Tantraloka is the basis of the upaya (means) of liberation. In the PGM, this is the 'silence of the initiate' — the secret of the unspoken name that is the 'seal' of the theurgic ascent. The 'four weeks' (the lunar month) that the brother 'has been within' the seeker's vicinity is the cycle of the moon — the measure of time in the subtle world, where the brother (the daimon) follows the rhythms of the sidereal consciousness rather than the solar calendar. Steiner describes this precisely: the higher self works upon the human being in the rhythms of the lunar cycle, which is the measure of the etheric body's receptivity to spiritual impression.

gaps: The seven Axiomata of the Fraternity and the detailed statutes need extraction
The examination scene with Iulianus de Campus and the threshold imagery need deeper treatment

issues.title: Passages are extracts from a multi-section treatise
issues.description: Schweighardt's text includes three chapters plus title-page symbolism. The seven axioms of the Fraternity are not yet extracted.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Schweighardt's Speculum Sophicum Rhodostauroticum. Six passages cover the Physiologia-Theologia syzygy, the invisible Collegium, and the inner path, with commentary connecting to Shakti-Shiva polarity, the invisible sangha, and Tantric sadhana.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from Schweighardt's Speculum
